analysis: Across the top heuristics a clear, reproducible pipeline emerges: fast Pareto/filtering → cheap proxy scoring (box‑volume ± scalarization via weight vectors) → small k‑proportional shortlist → greedy/CELF lazy greedy using cached singleton HVs (and selective pairwise caches) → bounded deterministic 1‑for‑1 local swaps → deterministic padding. Stronger variants tune shortlist size, scalarization richness, and HV‑call budgets; they also use box‑volume upper bounds to prune candidates. Weaker designs (e.g., GSI_LS) do many full HV recomputations in nested loops, lack caching, and perform expensive Python‑level membership/array copies repeatedly. Common avoidable overheads: repeated pg.hypervolume object construction and full hv recomputations, frequent vstack/copying, linear membership checks on lists (use sets), and overlarge shortlists. Pairwise caching helps only when shortlist is small; otherwise it wastes HV budget. CELF + cached singletons gives the best tradeoff between solution quality and HV calls; adaptive shortlist sizing and tight upper‑bound pruning control cost.
experience: Make a tight shortlist (k‑proportional), cache singleton HVs and selective pairwise HVs only when shortlist ≤ ~40, use box‑volume upper bounds to skip candidates, run CELF lazy greedy to minimize recomputations, cap HV calls and swaps, avoid costly per‑candidate array copies, and tune weight‑vector count adaptively. Deterministic seeds and set membership for O(1) checks improve reproducibility and speed.
current_reflection: Make a tight shortlist (k‑proportional), cache singleton HVs and selective pairwise HVs only when shortlist ≤ ~40, use box‑volume upper bounds to skip candidates, run CELF lazy greedy to minimize recomputations, cap HV calls and swaps, avoid costly per‑candidate array copies, and tune weight‑vector count adaptively. Deterministic seeds and set membership for O(1) checks improve reproducibility and speed.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.
bad_reflections: Prioritize a small, k‑proportional shortlist (Pareto-aware), use CELF lazy greedy with cached singleton HVs (add selective pairwise cache when shortlist is small), and prune candidates via cheap box-volume upper bounds. Vectorize dominance and proxy calculations, use sets for membership, limit exact HV calls with an explicit budget, and finish with a bounded deterministic 1‑for‑1 swap. These give reproducible, high‑quality HV subsets with controlled runtime.

Favor CELF lazy greedy + singleton (and selective pairwise) caching, adaptive shortlist sized by k+Pareto, strong cheap upper bounds to prune candidates, and incremental cached marginal updates for swaps. Cap HV calls, avoid dense weight sets unless budgeted, and prioritize deterministic tie‑breaks; these give the best quality/cost tradeoff.

Shortlist aggressively but proportionally to k, use CELF with cached singleton HVs and selective pairwise caching, apply cheap upper‑bound pruning (box volumes) before exact HV calls, enforce an HV‑call budget, and do a small bounded swap pass. Reuse arrays, avoid repeated full recomputations, and tune weight/vector counts to the cost‑benefit sweet spot for your problem size.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: k‑proportional shortlist; CELF lazy greedy; singleton + selective pairwise HV cache; box‑volume upper bounds; HV‑call cap; adaptive weight vectors; deterministic seeds; set membership.

- Advice: Build a Pareto‑aware shortlist proportional to k, run CELF with cached singleton HVs and enable pairwise caching only when shortlist ≤ 40; prune with box‑volume upper bounds; batch HV evaluations, reuse arrays, cap HV calls and perform a small bounded swap pass; tune weight‑vector count adaptively and seed RNGs for determinism.

- Avoid: Per‑candidate array copies, nested/redundant HV recomputations, unbounded dense weight sets, nondeterministic tie‑breaks, and skipping an explicit HV budget.

- Explanation: These choices minimize costly HV evaluations, keep runtime k‑proportional, improve reproducibility, and produce high‑quality subsets within a controlled cost.
